title: Lean Maturity Assessment
subtitle: Evaluate your factory across 12 lean dimensions based on the Shingo Model and Toyota Production System. 53 questions, about 15 minutes. Your results will reveal priority areas and recommended tools.
saveError: Failed to save assessment. Please try again.
recommendationsDesc: Categories are ranked from weakest to strongest. Focus on the top priorities first for maximum impact.
noHistory: No previous assessments found. Complete your first assessment to start tracking progress.
actionPlanDesc: Auto-generated recommendations for categories scoring below 3.0 (Standardized level). Focus on these areas for maximum lean maturity improvement.
noActionsNeeded: All categories are at Standardized level or above. Maintain your current practices and look for opportunities to advance to Managed and World Class levels.
levelAdHocDesc: Your factory has significant improvement opportunities. No structured lean approach is in place. Start with foundational practices for quick wins.
levelReactiveDesc: Basic processes exist but are inconsistent. You are beginning the lean journey. Structure and standardization will accelerate progress.
levelStandardizedDesc: Standardized processes are in place. Good foundation established. Focus on measurement, active management, and closing remaining gaps.
levelManagedDesc: Processes are measured and actively improved. Strong lean maturity. Sustain gains and pursue advanced optimization.
levelWorldClassDesc: Best-in-class continuous self-improvement culture. Benchmark performance. Focus on innovation, sharing best practices, and mentoring others.
catWorkplaceDesc: Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain, and Safety practices across the shop floor.
catVisualDesc: Daily management boards, Andon systems, KPI visibility, and standard work display on the shop floor.
catStandardDesc: Standard operating procedures, work instructions, job element sheets, and training within industry (TWI).
catImprovementDesc: PDCA cycles, suggestion systems, kaizen events, daily kaizen culture, and employee involvement.
catProblemDesc: 5-Why analysis, A3 thinking, root cause methodology, structured problem resolution, and follow-up.
catTPMDesc: Autonomous maintenance, planned maintenance, OEE tracking, and equipment reliability programs.
catFlowDesc: Value stream mapping, takt time adherence, one-piece flow, pull systems, and WIP management.
catQualityDesc: Built-in quality (jidoka), poka-yoke, statistical process control, FMEA, and inspection methods.
catSafetyDesc: Safety culture, hazard identification, incident management, near-miss reporting, and EHS compliance.
catLeadershipDesc: Gemba walks, leader standard work, coaching kata, respect for people, and lean leadership behaviors.
catSupplyDesc: Supplier development, kanban systems, inventory management, JIT delivery, and logistics optimization.
catDigitalDesc: Digital transformation, IoT sensors, data analytics, automation, and Industry 4.0 integration with lean practices.
wp1Q: How well is Sort (Seiri) practiced — are unnecessary items removed from work areas?
wp1D1: Work areas are cluttered with unnecessary items. No systematic removal of unneeded tools, materials, or equipment.
wp1D2: Occasional cleanouts happen but are not systematic. Items accumulate again quickly after cleanup campaigns.
wp1D3: Red-tag events are conducted periodically. Criteria for necessary vs. unnecessary items are documented.
wp1D4: Regular Sort audits ensure only needed items remain. Disposition processes are tracked and measured.
wp1D5: Sort is self-sustaining. Teams proactively manage inventory of items in their zones. Near-zero unnecessary items.
wp2Q: How effectively is Set in Order (Seiton) implemented — does everything have a designated place?
wp2D1: No designated locations for tools or materials. Operators spend significant time searching for what they need.
wp2D2: Some labeling exists but is inconsistent. Shadow boards or marked locations are rare.
wp2D3: Most work areas have labeled locations, shadow boards, and visual indicators for tools and materials.
wp2D4: Point-of-use storage is optimized for ergonomics and efficiency. Compliance is audited and tracked.
wp2D5: Layout optimization is continuous. Retrieval times are measured and minimized. Best practices shared across areas.
wp3Q: How consistently is Shine (Seiso) practiced — are work areas cleaned and inspected regularly?
wp3D1: Cleaning is reactive — done only when areas become visibly dirty or during external audits.
wp3D2: End-of-shift cleaning occurs sometimes but is not standardized. Equipment inspection during cleaning is rare.
wp3D3: Cleaning schedules exist with assigned responsibilities. Operators combine cleaning with basic equipment inspection.
wp3D4: Cleaning-inspection-lubrication-tightening (CILT) routines are standard. Abnormalities detected through cleaning are tracked.
wp3D5: Source of contamination elimination is pursued. Cleaning time is minimized through root-cause countermeasures.
wp4Q: How mature is the Sustain (Shitsuke) discipline — are 5S standards maintained consistently over time?
wp4D1: Previous 5S efforts have degraded. No accountability or follow-up system exists to maintain standards.
wp4D2: Supervisors occasionally remind teams about 5S, but there is no routine audit or accountability system.
wp4D3: Regular 5S audits are conducted (weekly/monthly). Scores are posted and reviewed in team meetings.
wp4D4: 5S audit trends are tracked. Corrective actions have owners and deadlines. Audit scores improve over time.
wp4D5: 5S is self-sustaining culture. Teams take ownership of their zones. Standards evolve through kaizen activities.
wp5Q: How well is Safety (the 6th S) integrated into your workplace organization program?
wp5D1: Safety is not considered part of workplace organization. Hazards in the work area are not systematically addressed.
wp5D2: Safety concerns are raised occasionally during 5S activities but not systematically addressed.
wp5D3: Safety checks are included in 5S audit forms. Hazard identification is part of the standard routine.
wp5D4: 6S program with safety as an explicit pillar. Safety hazards found during audits are tracked and resolved.
wp5D5: Safety is fully embedded in workplace organization. Zero-hazard zones. Teams proactively eliminate safety risks.
vm1Q: How effectively are daily management boards used on the shop floor?
vm1D1: No visual management boards exist. Performance information is only available in reports or computer systems.
vm1D2: Some boards exist but are not updated regularly. Information is often outdated or incomplete.
vm1D3: Daily management boards are in place with SQCDP categories. Updated at least once per shift.
vm1D4: Boards are updated in real time. Daily stand-up meetings use the boards. Actions are tracked to completion.
vm1D5: Tiered daily management from team to plant level. Escalation cascades are fast and effective. Digital and physical boards integrated.
vm2Q: How mature is the Andon system for signaling abnormalities?
vm2D1: No Andon system exists. Operators have no standard way to signal problems or request help.
vm2D2: Operators call for help verbally or by phone. Response times are unpredictable and often slow.
vm2D3: An Andon system (lights, buttons, or digital) is in place. Response roles are defined but not always followed.
vm2D4: Andon calls are logged, response times measured, and reviewed daily. Recurring calls trigger root-cause investigation.
vm2D5: Andon is embedded in the culture. Every team member uses it. Response is immediate. Data drives systemic improvement.
vm3Q: How visible are KPIs and performance metrics on the shop floor?
vm3D1: Operators have no visibility into production performance, quality, or safety metrics during their shift.
vm3D2: End-of-shift or end-of-day summaries are posted, but real-time visibility is lacking.
vm3D3: Key metrics (output, quality, safety) are displayed at each work area. Hourly tracking boards are in use.
vm3D4: Real-time dashboards show OEE, output vs. target, and alerts. Trends are visible and discussed daily.
vm3D5: Full transparency across all levels. Any employee can see plant-wide performance in real time. Predictive analytics displayed.
vm4Q: How well is standard work displayed and used as a visual management tool at workstations?
vm4D1: No standard work documents are displayed at workstations. Operators rely on memory or informal training.
vm4D2: Some work instructions are posted but are outdated or not followed. Visual work aids are rare.
vm4D3: Standard work combination sheets and job element sheets are displayed at key workstations.
vm4D4: Visual work standards are actively used for training and auditing. They are updated when processes change.
vm4D5: Dynamic visual standards (digital displays, video). Operators participate in creating and improving visual standards.
vm5Q: How well does the visual factory concept extend beyond the shop floor to offices and support areas?
vm5D1: Visual management is not used in offices or support areas. Only production has any visual indicators.
vm5D2: Some departments have whiteboards or status boards, but they are not standardized or regularly updated.
vm5D3: Support departments (maintenance, quality, logistics) have visual management boards aligned with production.
vm5D4: Enterprise-wide visual management. Office 5S and visual workflows. Cross-functional visibility of workload and status.
vm5D5: Seamless visual factory from shop floor to boardroom. Real-time digital dashboards connect all departments.
sw1Q: How well are standard operating procedures (SOPs) documented and maintained?
sw1D1: Critical knowledge lives in experienced operators' heads. If they leave, knowledge is lost.
sw1D2: Some SOPs exist but are outdated, incomplete, or stored in binders that no one reads.
sw1D3: SOPs are documented for key processes using standard templates. They are accessible at workstations.
sw1D4: SOPs are current, visual, and actively used. A revision process ensures updates when processes change.
sw1D5: Living standards created and improved by operators. Digital SOPs with video. Automatic revision tracking and compliance.
sw2Q: How consistently do operators follow standardized work procedures?
sw2D1: Wide variation exists in how different operators perform the same task. Quality depends on who is working.
sw2D2: Standards exist but adherence is inconsistent. Deviations are common and not tracked.
sw2D3: Most operators follow documented methods. Standardized work charts define sequence, timing, and WIP.
sw2D4: Standardized work adherence is regularly audited. Deviations are treated as improvement opportunities, not punishment.
sw2D5: Standardized work is the baseline for improvement. Operators improve standards through experimentation and validation.
sw3Q: How effectively are work instructions and training materials designed for the shop floor?
sw3D1: No visual work instructions exist. Training is done informally through shadowing with no structured content.
sw3D2: Text-heavy instructions exist but are not visual or easy to follow. Translation to multiple languages is missing.
sw3D3: Visual work instructions with photos/diagrams are available for key tasks. Job element sheets identify key points.
sw3D4: TWI (Training Within Industry) methodology is used. Job breakdown sheets capture key points and reasons why.
sw3D5: Multi-modal training (video, AR, digital). Training effectiveness is measured. Competency verification is rigorous.
sw4Q: How well are changeover/setup procedures standardized and optimized (SMED)?
sw4D1: Each operator does setups their own way. Changeover times are long, variable, and unpredictable.
sw4D2: Experienced operators share setup knowledge informally. No documented standard sequence exists.
sw4D3: Changeover steps are documented. Internal/external separation has begun. Standard sequence is defined.
sw4D4: SMED methodology is applied. Changeover times are tracked and targeted for reduction. Parallel tasks are optimized.
sw4D5: Single-minute changeovers achieved. One-touch or no-touch setups where possible. Changeover is a competitive advantage.
sw5Q: How well is Training Within Industry (TWI) or structured on-the-job training practiced?
sw5D1: No structured on-the-job training exists. New operators learn by watching and trial-and-error.
sw5D2: Experienced operators mentor new hires informally, but there is no standard training methodology.
sw5D3: TWI Job Instruction method is used for key operations. Trainers are certified and follow a standard process.
sw5D4: Training effectiveness is verified through competency assessments. Skill matrices track qualification status.
sw5D5: Comprehensive TWI program (JI, JM, JR). Every supervisor is a certified trainer. Training time is minimized through excellent methods.
ci1Q: How active is the employee suggestion/improvement idea system?
ci1D1: No formal system for improvement ideas. Workers feel their suggestions are not valued or heard.
ci1D2: A suggestion box exists but ideas are rarely reviewed, and feedback to submitters is inconsistent.
ci1D3: A structured suggestion system exists with regular review. Implementation rate is tracked but below 30%.
ci1D4: Ideas are reviewed weekly with fast feedback. Implementation rate exceeds 50%. Contributors are recognized.
ci1D5: Multiple ideas per employee per month. Ideas implemented within days. Culture of continuous daily improvement.
ci2Q: How frequently are structured Kaizen events (workshops) conducted?
ci2D1: No kaizen events have been conducted. Improvement happens only through individual initiative.
ci2D2: Occasional improvement projects occur but are not structured as kaizen events with clear methodology.
ci2D3: Kaizen events are held quarterly with cross-functional teams. Standard A3/PDCA methodology is used.
ci2D4: Monthly kaizen events target prioritized opportunities. Results are measured and sustained through follow-up.
ci2D5: Weekly kaizen activities are normal. Both rapid kaizen blitzes and longer-term improvement projects run in parallel.
ci3Q: How well is PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) applied as a standard improvement methodology?
ci3D1: Improvements are made without structured methodology. Changes are implemented without measuring results.
ci3D2: Some teams use PDCA informally, but the methodology is not standardized or required across the organization.
ci3D3: PDCA is the standard improvement methodology. Templates and training are available for all team members.
ci3D4: PDCA cycles are tracked to completion. Check and Act phases are rigorously followed with data verification.
ci3D5: PDCA is embedded in daily work. Nested PDCA cycles operate at team, department, and plant levels simultaneously.
ci4Q: How well are daily kaizen practices integrated into routine work?
ci4D1: No daily improvement activities exist. Teams focus solely on production output.
ci4D2: Some operators make small improvements on their own initiative, but there is no structured daily kaizen process.
ci4D3: Daily team meetings include brief discussion of improvement opportunities. Some actions are captured.
ci4D4: Structured daily kaizen with visual boards. Small improvements are tracked, measured, and shared across shifts.
ci4D5: Every team member contributes to daily kaizen. Improvement is integrated into standard work and measured at every level.
ci5Q: How well is improvement knowledge shared and deployed across the organization?
ci5D1: Improvements stay within the team that made them. No mechanism exists for sharing learnings across the organization.
ci5D2: Occasional sharing happens in management meetings, but no systematic horizontal deployment exists.
ci5D3: A yokoten (horizontal deployment) process exists. Best practices are documented and shared at regular intervals.
ci5D4: Cross-functional improvement sharing is routine. A best-practice database is maintained and actively used.
ci5D5: Rapid horizontal deployment culture. Improvements spread across all sites within days. Learning organization mindset.
ps1Q: How consistently is structured root cause analysis (5-Why, Ishikawa) applied to problems?
ps1D1: Problems are fixed symptomatically. No root cause investigation is performed. Issues recur frequently.
ps1D2: Root cause analysis happens for major issues but relies on experience rather than structured methodology.
ps1D3: 5-Why and Ishikawa methods are used regularly. Templates and training are available for problem solvers.
ps1D4: Root cause analysis is required for all significant problems. Quality of analysis is reviewed. Recurrence rate is tracked.
ps1D5: Deep root cause thinking is universal. Multiple methods are mastered. Near-zero recurrence of solved problems.
ps2Q: How effectively is A3 thinking used as a problem-solving and communication tool?
ps2D1: A3 reports are not used. Problems are discussed informally without structured documentation or follow-through.
ps2D2: Some A3 reports are written but the thinking process behind them is not well understood or practiced.
ps2D3: A3 reports are used for significant problems. The PDCA-based A3 process is taught and practiced.
ps2D4: A3 thinking is the standard problem-solving language. Mentoring through A3 develops capability across the organization.
ps2D5: A3 thinking is embedded in the culture. Used for strategy deployment, not just problem solving. Everyone speaks the A3 language.
ps3Q: How well are problems prioritized using data (Pareto analysis, trend data)?
ps3D1: Problems are addressed based on urgency or who complains loudest. No data-driven prioritization exists.
ps3D2: Some data is available but not used systematically to prioritize which problems to solve first.
ps3D3: Pareto analysis is used to identify top problems. Defect data is collected and categorized for analysis.
ps3D4: Data-driven prioritization is standard. Top problems are assigned to cross-functional teams with clear targets.
ps3D5: Predictive analytics identify emerging problems before they impact. Continuous Pareto reduction drives toward zero defects.
ps4Q: How effectively are corrective actions verified and sustained after problem solving?
ps4D1: Corrective actions are implemented but never verified. It is unknown whether problems were truly solved.
ps4D2: Some follow-up occurs but is informal. Problems often recur because solutions were not sustained.
ps4D3: A follow-up process exists with defined verification steps. Effectiveness checks are scheduled after implementation.
ps4D4: Verification is mandatory with data evidence. Horizontal deployment ensures solutions are applied where applicable.
ps4D5: Closed-loop system with automatic monitoring. Solutions are standardized and embedded in processes. Near-zero recurrence.
ps5Q: How well is the organization's problem-solving capability developed across all levels?
ps5D1: Problem solving relies on a few experienced individuals. No systematic capability development exists.
ps5D2: Some training in problem-solving methods exists but is not applied consistently on the shop floor.
ps5D3: Problem-solving training is part of the standard development program. Teams practice structured methods regularly.
ps5D4: Multi-level problem-solving capability. Team leaders, supervisors, and managers all use structured methods daily.
ps5D5: Everyone is a problem solver. Scientific thinking is the organizational norm. Problems are welcomed as improvement opportunities.
tp1Q: What is your overall maintenance strategy?
tp1D1: Equipment is fixed only when it breaks. There is no planned maintenance schedule or strategy.
tp1D2: Basic preventive maintenance exists for critical equipment, but most work is still reactive (run-to-failure).
tp1D3: Preventive maintenance schedules cover key equipment. PM compliance is tracked. Some condition monitoring exists.
tp1D4: Predictive and preventive maintenance are integrated. MTBF and MTTR are tracked per machine with improvement targets.
tp1D5: Full TPM with autonomous, planned, and predictive maintenance. Equipment reliability exceeds 95%. Zero-breakdown culture.
tp2Q: How well do operators perform autonomous maintenance (CILT — Clean, Inspect, Lubricate, Tighten)?
tp2D1: Operators do not perform any equipment care. All maintenance is handled by the maintenance department.
tp2D2: Operators do basic cleaning but no structured inspection, lubrication, or tightening routines.
tp2D3: CILT checklists exist for key equipment. Operators are trained and perform routines, but compliance varies.
tp2D4: CILT compliance is tracked and audited. Operators detect and report abnormalities. Standards are continuously improved.
tp2D5: Operators own their equipment. They perform advanced diagnostics, contribute to PM planning, and drive zero-defect goals.
tp3Q: How effectively is Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) tracked and used for improvement?
tp3D1: OEE is not measured. Equipment performance, uptime, and quality losses are not systematically tracked.
tp3D2: OEE is calculated occasionally or for some equipment, but data is not used for structured improvement.
tp3D3: OEE is tracked regularly for key equipment. The six big losses are categorized and reviewed monthly.
tp3D4: OEE drives focused improvement. Loss waterfall analysis identifies top losses. Improvement teams target specific loss categories.
tp3D5: Real-time OEE dashboards. OEE exceeds 85%. Losses are systematically eliminated through cross-functional focused improvement.
tp4Q: How well is equipment improvement and early equipment management practiced?
tp4D1: Equipment is purchased without considering maintenance needs or operator input. No lessons-learned process exists.
tp4D2: Some feedback is collected after equipment installation, but it rarely influences future purchases.
tp4D3: Equipment specifications include maintainability requirements. Operators and maintenance provide input during procurement.
tp4D4: Life-cycle cost analysis is standard. Lessons from existing equipment are systematically applied to new equipment design.
tp4D5: Early equipment management is integrated with TPM. New equipment achieves target OEE within days of installation.
tp5Q: How effectively is focused improvement applied to eliminate chronic equipment losses?
tp5D1: Chronic equipment losses are accepted as normal. No structured approach to eliminate recurring problems.
tp5D2: Major breakdowns are investigated, but chronic minor losses are not systematically addressed.
tp5D3: Focused improvement teams are formed to address top equipment losses identified through OEE analysis.
tp5D4: Systematic loss elimination using PM analysis and why-why analysis. Chronic losses are reduced year over year.
tp5D5: Near-zero chronic losses. Focused improvement is continuous and proactive. Equipment performance approaches theoretical capacity.
fl1Q: Has value stream mapping (VSM) been conducted for your key product families?
fl1D1: The concept of value stream mapping is unknown or not practiced. Material and information flows are not documented.
fl1D2: General awareness of process flow exists, but no formal value stream maps have been created.
fl1D3: Current-state VSMs exist for major product families. Value-add vs. non-value-add activities are identified.
fl1D4: Future-state VSMs drive improvement projects. VSMs are updated regularly. Lead time reduction is actively tracked.
fl1D5: VSM is a living management tool. Extended value stream includes suppliers and customers. Continuous flow improvements.
fl2Q: How well is takt time understood and used to pace production?
fl2D1: Takt time is not calculated or used. Production is pushed based on batch scheduling or forecast.
fl2D2: Takt time is known conceptually but not used to balance workloads or set production pace.
fl2D3: Takt time is calculated and displayed. Work content is roughly balanced to takt. Cycle time vs. takt is measured.
fl2D4: Production is paced to takt. Yamazumi (work balance) charts are used. Deviations trigger escalation.
fl2D5: Flexible takt time adjustment based on demand. Pitch-based scheduling with visual signals. Near-perfect work balance.
fl3Q: How mature are your pull systems (kanban, supermarkets, FIFO lanes)?
fl3D1: All production is push-based. WIP levels are uncontrolled and vary widely between workstations.
fl3D2: Some FIFO lanes exist between processes, but overall production scheduling is still push-based.
fl3D3: Kanban or supermarket systems exist for key materials. WIP limits are set but not always enforced.
fl3D4: Pull systems govern material flow. WIP limits are actively managed. Kanban quantities are regularly reviewed and optimized.
fl3D5: End-to-end pull from customer to supplier. Mixed-model leveled production (heijunka). Approaching one-piece flow.
fl4Q: How effectively is work-in-process (WIP) inventory managed and minimized?
fl4D1: WIP is not tracked. Large batches and uncontrolled inventory buffers exist between operations.
fl4D2: WIP is counted periodically but not actively managed. Batch sizes are based on tradition, not optimization.
fl4D3: WIP targets are established. Inventory between processes is visible and roughly controlled.
fl4D4: WIP levels are measured daily. Reduction targets exist and are tracked. Small-lot production is practiced.
fl4D5: Near-single-piece flow. WIP is minimal and precisely controlled. Quick changeovers enable small lot sizes.
qs1Q: How well is built-in quality (jidoka) practiced — detecting defects at the source?
qs1D1: Defects are found at final inspection or by customers. No in-process quality checks exist.
qs1D2: Some in-process inspection happens, but defects frequently pass to downstream operations before detection.
qs1D3: Quality gates exist between major process steps. Operators perform self-inspection using defined criteria.
qs1D4: Stop-and-fix culture is practiced. Operators are empowered and expected to stop production when defects are detected.
qs1D5: Jidoka is embedded in equipment and processes. Automatic detection and stop. Zero defects passed downstream.
qs2Q: How extensively are mistake-proofing devices (poka-yoke) used?
qs2D1: No poka-yoke devices are in use. Defect prevention relies entirely on operator attention and skill.
qs2D2: A few mistake-proofing devices exist but were installed reactively after major quality escapes.
qs2D3: Poka-yoke is systematically considered for new processes. Existing critical operations have mistake-proofing in place.
qs2D4: Poka-yoke effectiveness is validated and tracked. FMEA drives prioritization of mistake-proofing installation.
qs2D5: Comprehensive poka-yoke coverage. Devices are tested regularly. Culture of designing processes that cannot produce defects.
qs3Q: How mature is Statistical Process Control (SPC) or data-driven quality monitoring?
qs3D1: No statistical methods are used for quality monitoring. Inspection is the only quality control method.
qs3D2: Some data is collected but not analyzed statistically. Control charts are not used or not understood.
qs3D3: SPC charts are maintained for critical-to-quality characteristics. Operators are trained in basic SPC concepts.
qs3D4: Process capability (Cpk) is monitored. Out-of-control conditions trigger investigation. SPC drives process improvement.
qs3D5: Advanced SPC with automated data collection. Processes consistently exceed Cpk 1.67. Predictive quality monitoring.
qs4Q: How effectively are quality problems prevented through FMEA and proactive risk assessment?
qs4D1: No formal risk assessment for quality. Problems are only addressed after they occur.
qs4D2: FMEA is used occasionally, mainly for new product launches, but not systematically applied or updated.
qs4D3: Process FMEA is conducted for key processes. Risk priority numbers (RPN) drive action prioritization.
qs4D4: FMEA is a living document, updated with lessons learned. High-RPN items are systematically addressed and verified.
qs4D5: Proactive risk management is embedded in all processes. FMEA, DRBFM, and lessons-learned databases drive prevention.
sc1Q: How strong is the safety culture — do employees actively participate in safety improvement?
sc1D1: Safety is seen as a compliance requirement. No proactive safety behavior or employee engagement exists.
sc1D2: Safety rules exist but are followed inconsistently. Near-misses are not reported. Safety is not a daily conversation.
sc1D3: Safety training is regular. Near-miss reporting exists. Safety observations are conducted but follow-up is inconsistent.
sc1D4: Strong near-miss reporting culture. Behavioral safety observations are routine. Safety leads production meetings.
sc1D5: Zero-harm culture. Every employee is a safety leader. Industry-leading safety performance sustained over years.
sc2Q: How effective is hazard identification and risk assessment across the workplace?
sc2D1: Hazards are not systematically identified. Risk assessments are not performed or are outdated.
sc2D2: Risk assessments exist for some areas but are not regularly updated. Hazard identification is not proactive.
sc2D3: Job Safety Analysis (JSA) is conducted for key tasks. Risk assessments are reviewed periodically.
sc2D4: Comprehensive risk assessment program. Hierarchy of controls is applied. Workers participate in hazard identification.
sc2D5: Dynamic risk assessment embedded in daily work. Leading indicators drive prevention. Risks eliminated at source.
sc3Q: How well are safety incidents and near-misses investigated and learned from?
sc3D1: Incidents are reported for compliance but not investigated. No lessons-learned process exists.
sc3D2: Serious incidents are investigated, but near-misses are ignored. Root causes are often superficial (blame the worker).
sc3D3: Structured investigation process for incidents and near-misses. Root cause analysis is applied consistently.
sc3D4: Investigations lead to systemic fixes. Horizontal deployment of lessons learned. Leading indicators are tracked.
sc3D5: Predictive safety analytics. Near-miss ratio drives prevention. Cross-site learning. Extended time without recordable incidents.
sc4Q: How well are environmental, health, and safety (EHS) management systems integrated?
sc4D1: No formal EHS management system. Compliance is reactive and fragmented.
sc4D2: Basic EHS compliance exists but systems are not integrated. Environmental management is separate from safety.
sc4D3: EHS management system (e.g., ISO 14001/45001) is implemented. Policies and procedures are documented.
sc4D4: Integrated EHS with measurable targets. Regular audits and management reviews drive improvement.
sc4D5: EHS is a strategic priority. Sustainability goals integrated with lean. Industry leadership in environmental performance.
ld1Q: How frequently and effectively does leadership practice Gemba walks (go-and-see at the workplace)?
ld1D1: Leaders rarely visit the shop floor. Management decisions are made from the office without direct observation.
ld1D2: Leaders visit the floor when problems occur, but structured Gemba walks are not practiced.
ld1D3: Gemba walks are scheduled and occur regularly. Leaders observe, ask questions, and document findings.
ld1D4: Gemba walks are themed and structured. Findings drive improvement actions with clear ownership and follow-up.
ld1D5: Daily Gemba is a leadership habit. All levels participate. Gemba walks are coaching opportunities, not inspections.
ld2Q: How well is Leader Standard Work practiced by supervisors and managers?
ld2D1: Leaders have no standard routines. Their daily work is entirely reactive and driven by firefighting.
ld2D2: Some leaders have informal routines, but Leader Standard Work is not a formal practice.
ld2D3: Leader Standard Work is documented for supervisors. Key activities (audits, board reviews, coaching) are defined.
ld2D4: Leader Standard Work compliance is tracked. Leaders audit each other. Time allocation is measured and improved.
ld2D5: Leader Standard Work is embedded at all levels. Leaders spend 80%+ of time on value-adding leadership activities.
ld3Q: How well do leaders coach and develop their team members (coaching kata)?
ld3D1: Leaders primarily direct and control. Coaching is not part of the leadership approach.
ld3D2: Some informal mentoring occurs, but structured coaching methodology is not practiced.
ld3D3: Coaching kata or similar coaching methodology is introduced. Some leaders practice structured improvement coaching.
ld3D4: Regular coaching cycles are practiced. Leaders develop problem-solving capability in their teams through guided practice.
ld3D5: Coaching is the primary leadership method. Chain of coaching from plant manager to team leader. Coaching drives all improvement.
ld4Q: How deeply is 'Respect for People' embedded in the organizational culture?
ld4D1: Command-and-control culture. Workers are not consulted on decisions affecting their work. Ideas are not valued.
ld4D2: Management talks about respect but actions are inconsistent. Employee engagement is low.
ld4D3: Employee input is sought through formal channels. Teams participate in improvement activities. Respectful communication is expected.
ld4D4: True empowerment — teams make decisions about their work. Psychological safety is evident. Employee satisfaction is measured.
ld4D5: Deep mutual trust. Everyone's contribution is valued. Leaders serve their teams. Culture attracts and retains top talent.
sl1Q: How well are suppliers developed and integrated into your lean system?
sl1D1: Supplier relationships are purely transactional, based on lowest price. No supplier development activities exist.
sl1D2: Some preferred suppliers exist, but development is limited to quality complaints and corrective actions.
sl1D3: Key suppliers are evaluated regularly. Supplier development programs exist for critical suppliers. Joint improvement projects.
sl1D4: Strategic supplier partnerships. Suppliers participate in value engineering. Supplier scorecards drive improvement.
sl1D5: Extended lean enterprise. Suppliers are fully integrated into pull systems. Shared improvement goals and transparent communication.
sl2Q: How mature are your kanban and material replenishment systems?
sl2D1: Materials are ordered based on MRP push or when stockouts occur. No visual replenishment signals exist.
sl2D2: Some min/max inventory levels are set, but replenishment is still largely push-based and manual.
sl2D3: Kanban cards or electronic signals are used for key materials. Replenishment quantities are calculated and reviewed.
sl2D4: Integrated kanban system for most materials. Supplier kanban for key components. Inventory turns are tracked and improved.
sl2D5: Pull-based replenishment from supplier to point-of-use. Automated signals. Best-in-class inventory turns.
sl3Q: How effectively is inventory managed and waste in the supply chain eliminated?
sl3D1: Inventory levels are high and uncontrolled. Obsolete and excess stock is common. No waste analysis in logistics.
sl3D2: Inventory is counted periodically. Some efforts to reduce excess, but no systematic approach.
sl3D3: Inventory targets are set by category (raw, WIP, finished). ABC analysis guides management attention.
sl3D4: Inventory reduction is a strategic goal. Days of inventory is tracked and improved. Root causes of excess are addressed.
sl3D5: World-class inventory turns. Just-in-time delivery for most materials. Near-zero obsolescence. Supply chain VSM drives improvement.
dg1Q: How mature is your use of digital tools and IoT sensors on the shop floor?
dg1D1: No digital tools on the shop floor. All data collection is manual on paper forms.
dg1D2: Some digital tools exist (spreadsheets, basic software) but are disconnected and not integrated with production.
dg1D3: Digital data collection for key processes. Some IoT sensors monitor critical equipment parameters.
dg1D4: Integrated digital platform for production data. IoT sensors feed real-time dashboards. Data drives decisions.
dg1D5: Full Industry 4.0 integration. Digital twin, predictive analytics, AI-assisted decision making. Seamless data flow.
dg2Q: How effectively is data analytics used to drive lean improvement?
dg2D1: No data analytics capability. Decisions are based on intuition and experience alone.
dg2D2: Basic reports are generated but not used systematically for improvement. Data quality is poor.
dg2D3: Regular reports and dashboards support lean metrics (OEE, quality, delivery). Data is reliable and timely.
dg2D4: Advanced analytics identify patterns and opportunities. Statistical tools support process improvement efforts.
dg2D5: Predictive and prescriptive analytics drive continuous improvement. Machine learning optimizes processes in real time.
dg3Q: How well is automation integrated with lean principles rather than replacing them?
dg3D1: No automation strategy. Manual processes everywhere, or automation is implemented without lean thinking.
dg3D2: Some automation exists but was implemented without first stabilizing and standardizing the process.
dg3D3: Automation is applied after processes are lean and stable. The principle of 'simplify before automating' is followed.
dg3D4: Automation enhances lean flow. Cobots and flexible automation support operators. ROI is tracked and validated.
dg3D5: Seamless human-automation collaboration. Automation amplifies lean culture. Technology serves people and processes.
actionWorkplace: Implement 5S/6S program starting with a Red Tag event in the pilot area. Establish audit schedules and visual standards.
actionVisual: Install daily management boards with SQCDP categories. Implement hourly tracking and Andon response process.
actionStandard: Document SOPs for critical processes using visual templates. Implement TWI for standardized training transfer.
actionImprovement: Launch a suggestion system and schedule your first structured Kaizen event. Train teams on PDCA methodology.
actionProblem: Train all team leaders in 5-Why and A3 methodology. Require structured root cause analysis for all recurring problems.
actionTPM: Begin autonomous maintenance (CILT) on critical equipment. Start tracking OEE and the six big losses.
actionFlow: Conduct a current-state Value Stream Map for your main product family. Identify and prioritize waste elimination.
actionQuality: Implement quality gates between process steps. Deploy poka-yoke for top defect types. Start SPC on critical parameters.
actionSafety: Strengthen near-miss reporting culture. Conduct Job Safety Analysis for high-risk tasks. Review EHS management system.
actionLeadership: Establish Leader Standard Work for supervisors. Schedule structured Gemba walks. Train leaders in coaching kata.
actionSupply: Map material flow and implement kanban for key materials. Set inventory reduction targets. Develop key suppliers.
actionDigital: Assess digital readiness. Implement digital data collection for key processes. Start with OEE dashboards before pursuing advanced analytics.
